af_score_components.notes: Google Gemini CLI with MCP server support (quality 72). Flexible auth: OAuth for interactive, API key for simple agents, service account for production. Gemini 2.0 Flash is the cost-efficient default; Pro for complex reasoning. Rate limits vary by model and tier. MCP integration enables Gemini to use external tools. Context window up to 1M tokens is a key differentiator. Code execution sandbox available. File upload required for multimodal content. Good for agents that need to orchestrate other AI models.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Multiple auth methods — service account recommended for production agents. IAM scopes for Generative AI access. Google-maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance. API key or service account JSON via environment variables. Gemini API data used for model improvement unless opted out — review data policy for sensitive agent workflows. Vertex AI Gemini provides stricter data governance.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Google Cloud infrastructure with SLA. Stable API versioning. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. Rate limits vary by model — Flash less constrained than Pro. Streaming responses add reliability complexity for long-running generations. Context window limits require chunking strategy for very large inputs. Model availability changes as Gemini evolves.

af_score_components.notes: MCP server with limited quality (52). Workflow orchestration tool built on Gemini AI. Service account auth. Documentation is minimal — limited beyond basic setup. Rate limits inherited from Gemini API. Workflow definitions and flow management are the primary operations. Early-stage tool with active development — expect breaking changes. Depends on Gemini API availability. Useful for Gemini-centric workflow automation but not yet production-hardened.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS via Gemini API. Service account auth — good for agent workflows. IAM-scoped access to Gemini services. Unknown dependency hygiene for additional libraries. Service account JSON via environment variable. Security posture depends on Gemini API security model. Early-stage tool — security hardening may be incomplete.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted or GCP-based with no independent SLA. Unknown version — actively developed. No documented breaking change policy. Workflow execution depends on Gemini API availability. Retry behavior for failed workflow steps unclear. Early-stage reliability posture — significant operational risk for production workflows.

af_score_components.notes: Local MCP server for Ghidra (NSA's reverse engineering/binary analysis tool) (quality 75). No auth required — local Ghidra process. No rate limits (local tool). Enables LLM-assisted binary analysis: disassembly, decompilation, function analysis, cross-reference navigation. Ghidra must be running with the server plugin enabled. Very niche but high value for security research workflows. Error handling for invalid binary addresses/functions is limited. Excellent for CTF and malware analysis automation.

security_score_components.security_notes: Local MCP server — no network exposure by default. No credentials required or stored. Read-only binary analysis operations. Open source community-maintained — dependency hygiene depends on contributors. Analyzing malware via this tool requires sandboxed environment — never analyze untrusted binaries on production systems. macOS/Linux local access controls provide implicit security boundary.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Self-hosted — Ghidra must be running with plugin active. Version tied to Ghidra compatibility. No documented breaking change policy. Binary analysis queries are idempotent (same binary = same results). Ghidra memory usage can be significant for large binaries. Plugin must be reloaded after Ghidra updates. Useful tool for specialized workflows but production risk from upstream dependency.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Ghost CMS has two APIs: Content API (public, API key) and Admin API (JWT, for writing). Admin API JWT must be generated from API key using specific algorithm — not a standard JWT flow. Rate limits documented. Self-hosted vs Ghost(Pro) have different reliability profiles. Post slugs are auto-generated — agents must specify custom slugs for predictable URLs. Webhook support for content events. Good for content automation agents but JWT generation adds complexity.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS for Ghost(Pro); self-hosted TLS is operator's responsibility. Content API: API key. Admin API: JWT generated from Admin API key+secret pair. Admin/Content separation provides implicit scope isolation. Ghost Foundation maintained. No formal compliance certifications listed. Admin API key+secret must both be protected. JWT generation logic must be correctly implemented — errors lead to auth failures.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Ghost(Pro) has SLA; self-hosted operator-managed. Stable versioned API. Good migration guides. Retry guidance documented. JWT expiry (5-min default) requires frequent regeneration for long-running agents. Post creation not idempotent — check slug existence before creating. Webhooks require publicly accessible HTTPS endpoint. Content API fully idempotent.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. GitHub Actions REST API for workflow management. GITHUB_TOKEN (auto-provisioned in Actions context) or fine-grained PAT for external agents. Workflow runs are async — agents must poll run status. Rerun endpoints for failed jobs. Artifact download requires separate authenticated request. Rate limits clearly documented with response headers. Dispatch endpoints enable triggering workflows programmatically. Excellent automation surface for CI/CD orchestration agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. GITHUB_TOKEN with minimum required permissions + fine-grained PATs. Per-repository, per-workflow permissions on fine-grained PATs. GitHub (Microsoft) maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/FedRAMP compliance. GITHUB_TOKEN ephemeral (job scope); PAT via GitHub Secrets. OIDC federation available for keyless auth from cloud providers. Excellent security architecture.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: GitHub SaaS with SLA and comprehensive status page. Stable versioned API with excellent backward compatibility. Advance deprecation notices. Retry guidance with x-ratelimit-* headers. Async workflow runs require polling — implement with appropriate timeouts. Artifact storage limited by plan — monitor usage. Secondary rate limits (concurrent requests) require request serialization for bulk operations.

af_score_components.notes: Official GitHub MCP server (quality 85) — high quality with broad GitHub API coverage. OAuth or fine-grained PAT. Covers repos, PRs, issues, code search, branches, commits, files. Rate limits inherited from GitHub REST API. File operations require base64 encoding. PR creation is not idempotent — check for existing PRs. Code search has separate rate limits (10/min unauthenticated, 30/min authenticated). Excellent for GitHub-centric workflow agents.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. OAuth + fine-grained PAT with per-repo, per-operation permissions. GitHub-maintained official server. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/FedRAMP compliance. OAuth token or GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN via environment variable. Fine-grained PATs enable read-only repo access for safe agent operations. OIDC federation available for keyless workflows. Excellent security posture.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: GitHub SaaS infrastructure with SLA. Stable MCP server versioning. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. Rate limits from GitHub API — primary and secondary limits. PR/issue creation not idempotent — check existence before creating. Code search rate limits (30/min) require throttling for search-heavy workflows. MCP server is official — coordinated updates with GitHub API changes.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server (use github-mcp-server instead). Gold-standard REST API documentation — best-in-class developer experience. Fine-grained PATs are the preferred auth for agents (per-repo, per-operation permissions). Rate limits very clearly documented with real-time headers (x-ratelimit-*). Conditional requests with ETags for efficient polling. GraphQL API also available for complex queries. Webhooks for event-driven agents. REST endpoints for every GitHub operation. The benchmark for API quality.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Fine-grained PATs with per-repo, per-resource-type permissions. GITHUB_TOKEN scoped to repository in Actions. GitHub (Microsoft) maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR/FedRAMP compliance. Secrets via GitHub Secrets; PAT via environment variable. OIDC federation for keyless auth from cloud providers. Webhook signature verification required. Best-in-class permission granularity for developer platform APIs.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: GitHub SaaS with SLA and comprehensive status page. Exceptional API stability — backward compatibility maintained for years. Advance deprecation with migration guides. x-ratelimit-* headers enable precise backoff. Conditional requests (If-None-Match) minimize quota consumption. Secondary rate limits for concurrent requests require serialization. Pagination with Link headers. Gold standard for REST API reliability.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Comprehensive DevOps platform API. PAT with fine-grained scopes or OAuth2. Group and project access tokens for scoped automation. CI/CD, merge requests, issues, packages, registry all accessible. Rate limits documented (varies by plan). GraphQL API also available. Runner management and pipeline triggering primary agent use cases. Both SaaS and self-hosted support. Excellent for GitLab-centric DevOps automation.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS for GitLab.com; self-hosted TLS is operator's responsibility. PAT with fine-grained scopes (api, read_api, read_repository, write_repository, etc.). Group and project access tokens for scoped automation. GitLab maintained. SOC2/ISO27001/GDPR compliance. PAT via environment variable. Token expiry enforcement available. Service accounts for machine-to-machine workflows. Good scope granularity for least-privilege agents.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: GitLab.com SaaS with SLA; self-hosted operator-managed. Stable versioned API across minor versions. Excellent migration guides for breaking changes. Retry guidance documented. Rate limits by plan — higher for paid tiers. Cursor-based pagination. Pipeline trigger idempotency via trigger tokens. Async operations (pipelines, imports) require polling. Self-hosted version lag from SaaS adds compatibility consideration.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Enterprise search and knowledge discovery platform. Username/password + JWT is a poor auth pattern for agents — brittle and security-risky. API documentation is enterprise-gated and limited publicly. Rate limits undocumented publicly. Deployment is enterprise-specific with custom configurations. Connector-based content indexing. Agent integration requires significant setup with account team. Low scores reflect documentation gaps and poor agent auth patterns.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. Username/password + JWT is weak for agent auth — credentials at rest risk. No documented scope granularity. Enterprise SaaS — SOC2 likely but not publicly documented. Password storage in environment variables is poor practice. No service account pattern documented for agents. Enterprise support required to configure proper agent auth. High auth complexity reduces overall security posture.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: Enterprise SaaS with implicit SLA (no published metrics). Unknown API version stability. No public breaking change policy. Limited error recovery guidance. Auth fragility (password expiry, JWT refresh) creates reliability risk for long-running agents. Enterprise procurement required — not accessible for experimentation. Documentation gaps make reliability assessment difficult.

af_score_components.notes: No MCP server. Domain registration and DNS management API. API key + secret required together. Domain availability, registration, DNS record management are primary agent use cases. Domain registration is irreversible without explicit release. DNS propagation after changes takes time. Rate limits documented. Reseller vs standard API differences require correct endpoint selection. Webhook support limited. Good for domain management automation but high blast radius on registration operations.

security_score_components.security_notes: HTTPS enforced. API key + API secret pair — both required for auth. No scope granularity — full account access per key pair. GoDaddy maintained. No formal compliance certifications publicly listed. Both key and secret via environment variables. Domain registration and DNS changes have significant real-world impact — high blast radius. No read-only key option available.

reliability_score_components.reliability_notes: SaaS with status page. Stable versioned API. Good backward compatibility. Retry guidance documented. DNS record creation idempotent (PUT operations). Domain registration not idempotent — check availability before registering. DNS propagation not instant — agents must wait for TTL expiry after changes. Rate limits apply — domain availability checks can be constrained in bulk.
